Timish — Time Awareness App

UX Design  ·  Mobile App  ·  2024
UX Designer (Solo)
iOS Mobile App
Figma, FigJam

Overview

A time-management application designed to help users develop time awareness, reduce overwhelm, and manage tasks visually. Instead of showing time as numbers, Timish makes time felt — through colours and bars that fill as focus sessions progress.

Research Sources

  • UX Case Study on time-management struggles (UX Design magazine)
  • Time-related challenges in ADHD/LD populations (PMC / NCBI research)
  • General time-management strategies (University of Georgia field report)

Target Audiences

  • Students with executive function challenges
  • Adults with ADHD / Learning Disabilities
  • Adults with Executive Function Weakness regardless of age or occupation

Key Themes Identified

  • Anxiety and digital overload
  • Distractions and lack of organisation
  • Poor time awareness — time blindness
  • Weak planning and executive function
  • Overwhelm from complex interfaces

Key Observations

  • Time-management struggles have cognitive and neurobiological roots — not laziness
  • Students who struggle often become adults with similar challenges
  • The deeper problem is Executive Function Weakness, not age or occupation
  • Users require visual structure, reduced cognitive load, and simplified tools

Competitor Analysis

  • Tiimo — AI planner + focus mode, but too many options and overwhelming
  • One Thing — Simple to-do + focus, but interface not intuitive enough
  • Forest — Simple with reward system, but limited planning features
  • Interval Timer — Work–rest cycles, but not made for daily planning
  • Scheduley — Reminders + motivation, but motivation alone is not persistent

Competitor Insights

  • Need a persistent visible indicator for time awareness
  • Minimising text improves clarity significantly
  • Reduce user actions per session
  • Hide or avoid overwhelming settings
  • Use visual time rather than verbal time

The Problem

Users frequently lose track of time and forget deadlines because of time blindness, making it difficult to plan ahead, pace themselves, or stay aware of upcoming commitments. This results in missed deadlines and last-minute pressure.

Root cause: their brain processes planning, attention, and task initiation differently due to executive function difficulties. Current apps overwhelm them with text, options, and complex interfaces.

User Personas

  • Saara (20, Student) — Executive dysfunction, overwhelmed by text-heavy apps. Needs visual planning, advance reminders, and easy task initiation.
  • Arjun (30, Corporate Professional) — Inconsistent performance, loses track of time. Needs structured yet flexible reminders.
  • Riya (40, Business Owner) — Overwhelmed by multi-tasking, energy fluctuates. Needs prioritisation and daily clarity.

How Might We Questions

  • How might we help users start tasks easily without friction?
  • How might we use visuals to reduce overwhelm?
  • How might we reduce cognitive load during routine tasks?
  • How might we help users stay consistent without relying on motivation?
  • How might we simplify planning for multi-taskers?
  • How might we support productivity during low-energy days?

Rapid Ideation

Using FigJam sticky notes to explore ideas around visual awareness, minimal interfaces, time-based colour systems, and T-shirt size task arrangements.

The Solution — Time Bar System

Each hour of the day is represented by a bar. All bars start grey. When a focus session or task begins, bars fill dynamically with colours representing duration and focus type.

  • Default State — All bars start grey
  • Focus Mode — Pressing Focus fills the bar in real time
  • Task Mode — Adding a task changes bar colour based on task duration (S/M/L/XL)
  • Current Bar Indicator — Running bar appears as a smaller progress bar at the top

"Time in this app isn't shown as numbers — it's felt through colours. Each bar becomes a visual block of energy and attention."

App Screens

Process

Research → Target Audience Definition → Theme Identification → Competitor Analysis → Problem Statement → User Personas → POV Statements → HMW Questions → Rapid Ideation → Wireframes → High-Fidelity Design → Prototype

Catlop — Data-Driven Document Automation

UX Research  ·  User Flows  ·  SaaS  ·  2024
UX Research & User Flow
SaaS Web App
Figma, Notion

Overview

Catlop is a data-driven design tool that generates professionally formatted documents — catalogs, proposals, pitch decks, brochures — without requiring design skills. It bridges InDesign automation, Canva ease, and Figma theme control, powered by Shopify and CSV data sync.

The Market Gap

  • DIY tools (Canva, PowerPoint) are time-consuming with inconsistent results
  • Professional agencies cost $500–$5,000+ per catalog with slow turnaround
  • Enterprise software has steep learning curves unsuitable for SMBs
  • No existing tool solves data-to-design automation for non-designers at scale

Key Persona — Farhan

A seasonal retail shopkeeper in a Tier 2 city. Sources clothes from wholesalers, sells via WhatsApp and Instagram, and currently waits 2–3 days for a designer to create catalogs every season. Needs to upload product photos, auto-generate prices, and publish instantly.

My Contribution

  • Competitor analysis across 9 tools (Flipsnack, Canva, Catalog Machine, DCatalog, Burfy, Pomelli)
  • Defined market positioning and the middle-market gap
  • Created primary user persona with full pain point mapping
  • Designed three core user flows: Template-First, Data Flow, and AI Flow
  • Documented product scope across B2B, B2C, and enterprise segments

Three Core User Flows

  • Template-First Flow — Browse → Install → Edit → Export
  • Data Flow — Upload products/photos → Map to layout → Export
  • AI Flow — Prompt → AI generates template → Refine → Export
← Work

Wraft — Product Documentation Design System

Design Systems  ·  UI/UX  ·  2024
Wraft
Design System
Design System Designer
SaaS Web App
Figma

Overview

Wraft allows users to generate professionally formatted PDF documents using customisable templates. My role was to create a comprehensive design system ensuring visual consistency, scalability, and smooth developer handoff across the entire product.

My Work

  • Defined color tokens, typography scale, and spacing system
  • Built a component library in Figma (buttons, inputs, modals, cards, tables)
  • Created pattern guidelines for the document template editor UI
  • Audited and fixed existing design system for consistency
  • Restructured user flows for improved clarity and developer handoff

Note

Wraft is currently pre-launch. Screenshots will be added upon product release.

About

Hi, I'm Nashiba — Product Designer, AWS Solutions Architect, and multi-skilled creative who thrives at the intersection of empathy, tech, and purpose.

My career has been anything but ordinary — and that's what makes it powerful. I've worked as a DevOps engineer, freelance web designer, tutor, travel coordinator, social media manager, influencer marketing coordinator, and even spent time in modelling and content creation.

I'm also a parent to a neurodivergent child — a role that has taught me more about design, patience, and human needs than any textbook ever could.

What ties it all together? A deep belief that every role I've held has been a form of design — whether guiding a student, building a UI, managing a brand, or parenting with care. ✨ I design for clarity, inclusion, and real-life impact.

Certifications

Google UX Design Professional Certificate AWS Solutions Architecture Associate Microsoft Azure Administrator (AZ-104) Red Hat Certified System Administrator

Design Thinking

My Design Philosophy

As a UI/UX Product Designer, I believe good design starts with understanding people. Every product exists to solve a real problem. My role as a designer is to observe, question, and translate user needs into experiences that feel simple, intuitive, and efficient.

I focus on three core principles:

User Empathy

Understanding user behaviour, challenges, and motivations before making any design decisions.

Clarity over Complexity

Designing interfaces that feel natural and easy to navigate, especially for first-time users.

Purposeful Decisions

Every layout, colour, interaction, and component should support the user's goal — nothing arbitrary.

For me, product design is not just about creating screens — it is about designing experiences that make technology more accessible and useful for people.

How I Explain UX to Non-Designers

I like to explain UX design with a simple analogy: imagine building a house.

An architect designs the blueprint — deciding where rooms should be, how people will move through the space, where doors and windows go. Engineers and builders construct the house. But the most important person is the one who will live in it — they have specific needs around how the kitchen is arranged, where parking should be, the direction of the balcony.

UX design works in a similar way. Designers create the blueprint for digital products — how the product is structured, how users move through it, and how easy it is to use. At every stage, the designer asks: "Does this make sense for the user?"

Because ultimately, good design is not about the product itself — it's about how people experience it.

"I believe good design should feel simple, inclusive, and accessible — especially for people who may not be comfortable with technology."
